blocks.body: Future Simple: Time Expressions — tomorrow, next week, soon, later, one day, and other future markers.

{"level":"A1–A2","intro":"Words such as tomorrow, next week, soon, later, one day specify future time and often occur with will + V1.","rules":[{"label":"Tomorrow / next...","color":"emerald","text":"They point to a future day or period.","example":"I'll call tomorrow. / We'll travel next month."},{"label":"Soon / later","color":"blue","text":"They indicate near or later future without an exact date.","example":"She'll be here soon. / I'll finish it later."},{"label":"In + period","color":"amber","text":"It shows how long from now something will happen.","example":"The lesson will start in ten minutes."}]}

{"title":"1. Useful time expressions","intro":"Learn the most common future-time markers.","items":[{"label":"Tomorrow","title":"tomorrow / tomorrow morning","subtitle":"I'll see you tomorrow."},{"label":"Next...","title":"next week / month / year","subtitle":"They'll move next year."},{"label":"Soon / later","title":"soon / later / one day","subtitle":"You'll understand one day."},{"label":"In + period","title":"in an hour / in two days","subtitle":"He'll return in two days."}]}

{"title":"2. How these markers work","sections":[{"label":"Position","color":"emerald","description":"A marker often comes at the end, but it can also open the sentence.","examples":[{"en":"I'll call you tomorrow. / Tomorrow, I'll call you.","ua":"Both positions are possible."}]},{"label":"Time from now","color":"blue","description":"In + period means how long from now, not duration.","examples":[{"en":"The train will leave in five minutes.","ua":"Five minutes from now."}]},{"label":"Not an automatic rule","color":"amber","description":"A future marker does not always require will; plans and timetables may use other forms.","examples":[{"en":"I'm going to study tomorrow. / The train leaves at six tomorrow.","ua":"A plan and a timetable."}]}]}

{"title":"3. Typical combinations","intro":"Match each marker with a natural example.","rows":[{"en":"I'll phone you tomorrow.","ua":"the next day","note":"tomorrow"},{"en":"We'll probably know next week.","ua":"the following week","note":"next week"},{"en":"She'll be back soon.","ua":"in a short time","note":"soon"},{"en":"The meeting will start in ten minutes.","ua":"ten minutes from now","note":"in + period"}],"warning":"In this use, in two days means two days from now, not for two days."}

{"title":"4. Short summary","items":["Common markers: tomorrow, next..., soon, later, one day.","In + period shows how long from now.","A marker may come at the beginning or end.","Choose will by meaning; a future marker can also occur with be going to, Present Continuous, or a timetable Present Simple."]}

{"title":"5. Practice","select_title":"Exercise 1. Choose the correct option","select_intro":"Choose the natural future-time sentence.","selects":[{"label":"a) I'll call you tomorrow. / b) I'll call you yesterday.","prompt":"Which option is correct?","answer":"a"},{"label":"a) She'll return in two days. / b) She'll return two days ago.","prompt":"Which option is correct?","answer":"a"}],"options":["a","b"],"input_title":"Exercise 2. Build the sentence","input_intro":"Put the words in the correct order.","inputs":[{"before":"tomorrow / you / call / I'll","after":"→","answer":"I'll call you tomorrow."},{"before":"in an hour / start / will / it","after":"→","answer":"It will start in an hour."}]}
